questions.question: Two hosts on the same Wi-Fi both claim 'laptop.local' via mDNS. What must happen?
options: A. The host with the lowest IP wins
B. Both detect the conflict during the probe phase (3 queries 250 ms apart) and MUST pick a new name per RFC 6762 sec. 9.2
C. The kernel randomly picks one
D. Both keep the name; the last writer wins
questions.explanation: RFC 6762 sec. 9.2: 'If a host receives a response to a probe for a name, it MUST NOT use that name.' The standard Linux avahi-daemon implements this by appending -2, -3, etc. To defend, both hosts would need to be on the same IP (impossible) and have a strong reason to believe the other answer is stale (RFC 6762 sec. 9.3).

questions.question: What multicast group and port does mDNS use?
questions.explanation: mDNS uses 224.0.0.251 on IPv4 and ff02::fb on IPv6, port 5353, with IP TTL / hop limit 255 to ensure packets do not leak across a router. The TTL is the link-local scope boundary.

questions.question: An mDNS SRV record has TTL 4500 s. A printer is unplugged and a new printer at the same name is plugged in. How long until stale cache entries expire, and what is the corrective action?
options: B. 4500 s, send a cache-flush query (RFC 6762 sec. 10.2) to force peers to replace the entry
questions.explanation: RFC 6762 sec. 10 specifies the standard mDNS TTLs: A/AAAA 75 s, SRV 4500 s, PTR/TXT 120 s. Until the TTL elapses, clients resolve the old printer. The corrective action is a cache-flush query (RFC 6762 sec. 10.2) which carries a bit in the response telling peers to replace, not merge, cache entries.
